title: read_text_file: unbounded fs::read_to_string enables OOM via /dev/zero or oversized files
description:
Steps to reproduce:
1. 

Expected: 
Actual: 

**Dimension:** Security and Access / Performance

**Location:** /home/doll/OpenClaudia/src/tools/file/read.rs line 289 (read_text_file), line 41 (read_image_file), line 178 (read_notebook_file)

**Violation:**
read_text_file calls fs::read_to_string(path) which slurps the ENTIRE file into memory before the offset/limit slicing at lines 295-299. Consequences:
1. read_file on /dev/zero, /dev/random, /proc/kcore allocates until OOM-kill.
2. A legitimate 10 GB log file causes a multi-second freeze and virtual alloc spike even if the caller asked for limit=10 lines — the offset/limit is applied AFTER the full read.
3. The truncation guard at line 320 ('if result.len() > 100_000') fires AFTER allocation — useless for preventing OOM.
4. read_image_file (line 41) fs::read()s the whole file before base64-encoding — a 2 GB png triggers a 2.7 GB base64 allocation.
5. read_notebook_file (line 178) fs::read_to_string is equally unbounded.

**Mandated Refactor:**
1. fs::metadata(path) first; reject files whose len() exceeds MAX_READ_BYTES (e.g. 50 MiB). Return a helpful error directing the model to use offset+limit or grep.
2. If limit is specified, switch to BufReader::new(fs::File::open(path)?) and read line-by-line with .lines().skip(offset).take(limit), bounding memory to O(limit * max_line_len).
3. Reject special files at the perimeter: metadata.file_type().is_file() — not is_fifo, not char_device. Use std::os::unix::fs::FileTypeExt on Unix. This alone stops /dev/zero attacks.
4. Apply identical metadata-first checks to read_image_file and read_notebook_file.
5. Add image dimension/size cap — reject images >5 MB before base64-encoding.
